coredata.dc:title: Water-soluble luminescent gold nanoclusters reduced and protected by histidine for sensing of barbaloin and temperature
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shen
Bai, Meiqi
Qian, Jing
Guo, Yuyu
coredata.dc:description: In terms of the vital functions of barbaloin, it is necessary to develop a selective and sensitive detection means in barbaloin sensing to replace the previous defective analytical methods. Herein, the water-soluble luminescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) with good emission character were firstly established for barbaloin detection with high efficiency. The small size and stable probe was synthesized through one-pot way by protecting and reducing of histidine (His). The characterization results revealed that the quenching mechanism was mainly attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). The His-Au NCs displayed high selectivity to barbaloin against interferes and a good linear relationship existed in the range of 0.5–150 μM. More importantly, the probe was successfully employed to detect barbaloin in bovine serum samples with satisfactory recoveries and the excellent performance was also obtained from temperature sensing. This novel detection platform expanded the analytical methods for barbaloin based on fluorescence properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoclusters
Histidine
Barbaloin
Inner filter effect
Temperature sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of barbaloin in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of His-Au NCs; (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of His-Au NCs under different excitation wavelength from 340 nm to 385 nm.
(A) FT-IR spectra of His and His-Au NCs; (B) The XPS spectrum of Au 4f; (C) The TEM image of His-Au NCs.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of His-Au NCs in the presence of different barbaloin concentrations (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140 and 150 μM); (B) The linear fitting curve of 0.5–150 μM.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of barbaloin, His-Au NCs and His-Au NCs/barbaloin system; (B) Fluorescence decay curves of His-Au NCs in the absence (black) and presence (red) of barbaloin; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of barbaloin, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of His-Au NCs; (D) Suppressed efficiency of observed (black, Eobsd) and corrected (red, Ecor) fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs for detecting barbaloin.
Comparison of different analytical methods for barbaloin detection.
Recovery studies of barbaloin in bovine serum samples detected by His-Au NCs.

head.ce:title: Water-soluble luminescent gold nanoclusters reduced and protected by histidine for sensing of barbaloin and temperature

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, Shanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Taiyuan Normal University, Jinzhong 030619, Shanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, Shanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Arts, Taiyuan University of Technology, Jinzhong 030600, Shanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In terms of the vital functions of barbaloin, it is necessary to develop a selective and sensitive detection means in barbaloin sensing to replace the previous defective analytical methods. Herein, the water-soluble luminescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) with good emission character were firstly established for barbaloin detection with high efficiency. The small size and stable probe was synthesized through one-pot way by protecting and reducing of histidine (His). The characterization results revealed that the quenching mechanism was mainly attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). The His-Au NCs displayed high selectivity to barbaloin against interferes and a good linear relationship existed in the range of 0.5–150 μM. More importantly, the probe was successfully employed to detect barbaloin in bovine serum samples with satisfactory recoveries and the excellent performance was also obtained from temperature sensing. This novel detection platform expanded the analytical methods for barbaloin based on fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:para: As the important constituents of many Aloe species, barbaloin possesses superior anti-oxidative and anti-inflammatory activities [1–3]. Furthermore, barbaloin performs well in the resistance to several kinds of neoplastic cells and virus, immunomodulatory and protection of liver [4–8]. Considering the vital functions, one analytical method in barbaloin sensing with high selectivity and sensitivity is urgently needed.

According to the previous reports, different methods were used for barbaloin detection. Gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS) [9] in ElSohly’s work, high performance thin-layer chromatography (HPTLC) method in Coran’s [10] paper and ultra-high performance liquid chromatography with tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC-MS/MS) in Niu’s [11] investigation were typical examples. Although the above methods possessed high sensitivity and selectivity, the shortcomings of high cost, time consuming and complicated procedure [9–11] always limited their application. With developments, fluorescence analysis with low cost, high sensitivity and easy operation [12–14] successfully overcame the mentioned disadvantages of other techniques. As far as we knew, research about the fluorescence detection of barbaloin was not reported. So it is highly meaningful to construct sensors for the detection of barbaloin in the analytical areas. The sensitive probe and simple preparation methods were the key parameters.

Owing to the special photoluminescence properties, metal nanoclusters were widely used in optical sensing and imaging as emerging materials [15,16]. Among them, gold nanoclusters (Au NCs) attracted more attentions because of the excellent stability, biocompatibility and low toxicity [17–19]. Typically, the chemical reduction method was always adopted to prepare Au NCs, in which gold cations were reduced by deoxidizer and stabilized by organic ligand compounds [20,21]. In recent researches, many ligands of protein, enzyme, amino acids or other molecules [22–27] were used for the synthesis of fluorescent gold nanoclusters. The synthesized Au NCs were applied in many fields, such as catalysis, biosensing and imaging techniques [28–30].

As the basic constituent of protein, amino acid was regarded as the ideal ligands for preparing metal nanoclusters [31]. As a common amino acid, the histidine was proved to be a superior stabilizer for Au NCs. Hamzeh et al. [32] synthesized histidine capped-gold nanoclusters and the probe was successfully used for fluorescence detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide. Mohammad et al. [33] prepared an excellent genosensor on the surface of low-toxic substrate (gold nanoparticles) supported histidine and the results showed that the nano-Au-Hist provide suitable active sites for the DNA probe immobilization. Liu et al. [31] synthesized histidine-functionalized single-crystalline gold nanoparticles through hydrothermal method which possessed pH-dependent UV absorption characteristic.

Considering the above facts, our goal was to develop histidine-stabilized gold nanoclusters (His-Au NCs) for the fluorescence detection of barbaloin. The optical properties and structures were analysed by fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy (UV–vis), Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and fluorescence lifetime analysis. Furthermore, the stability of His-Au NCs was studied by testing the influences of storage time, UV irradiation time, ion strength and solvents on the fluorescence intensity. More importantly, the successful application in detecting barbaloin in bovine serum samples with high sensitivity and selectivity was developed. The schematic illustration about the synthesis of His-Au NCs and the detection of barbaloin was described in Scheme S1.

The fluorescence tests were measured on F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo Japan, 400 V, Ex/Em slits of 10.0/10.0 nm). The UV-vis absorption spectra were obtained by Shimadzu 2450 UV-visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images came from FEI Tecnai G2 F20 (United States, 200 kV). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured on the NICOLET360 spectrometer by using KBr pellets (4000–400 cm−1). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were derived from ESCALAB 250XI (United States). The pH values were measured by pH meter (FE20, Shanghai Mettler Instrument Company, Ltd., China).

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2·6H2O), calcium chloride dehydrate (CaCl2·2H2O), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O), manganese chloride (MnCl2), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate solution (NaHCO3), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4), glutamic acid (Gla), tyrosine (Tyr), proline (Pro), tryptophan (Trp), methionine (Met), cysteine (Cys), histidine (His), valine (Val), glutathione (GSH), Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA), carbamide (Car), hydrogen peroxide (HP), glucose (Glu) and barbaloin (Bar) were obtained from Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water (18.25 M·cm−1) was purified through Millipore Milli-Q system.

The Au NCs were synthesized through one-pot method in accordance with the previous research [34]. Firstly, 3 mL HAuCl4 (10 mM) was mixed with 9 mL histidine (0.1 M) in the bottle. Then, after incubation for 2 h at room temperature, the colour of solution transformed from yellow to pale yellow. The green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light illustrated the successful synthesis of Au NCs. The obtained His-Au NCs was kept at 4 °C for further use.

Different concentrations of barbaloin (0, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 7, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 110, 120, 130, 140 and 150 μM) were added into the mixture solution of 1 mL His-Au NCs solution and 1 mL PBS solution (pH = 7.0) for barbaloin detection. After incubation for 2 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured. To investigate the selectivity for barbaloin sensing, the influences of various possible interferes (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, Gla, Tyr, Pro, Trp, Met, Cys, His, Val, GSH, BSA, Car, HP and Glu) on the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs were tested.

The detection experiments of His-Au NCs towards barbaloin in real samples were performed in diluted bovine serum samples to study the practicability. The addition of different concentrations of barbaloin (5, 10, 15 and 20 μM) into the bovine serum samples was used to obtain the recovery results.

The optical properties of His-Au NCs were analysed by fluorescence spectra and the results were shown in Fig. 1 A. The His-Au NCs emitted the maximum fluorescence intensity at 465 nm when the excitation peak was located at 371 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra of His-Au NCs under different excitation wavelengths were displayed in Fig. 1B. With the excitation wavelengths increasing from 340 to 385 nm, the maxima and positions of emission peaks changed correspondingly. The above phenomenon was named as the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties which was the typical characteristics of most fluorescence nanoclusters [35].

Some techniques of FT-IR, XPS and TEM were carried out to investigate the morphology and structures of His-Au NCs. FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2 A were used to confirm whether the functional group of His appeared on the Au NCs surface. The nearly identical absorbance peak positions illustrated the appearance of the characteristic peaks of His for His-Au NCs [31]. The decreased peak intensity of His-Au NCs in comparison to His stated the interaction between His and Au NCs [36–38]. The chemical status of gold in His-Au NCs was derived from XPS results. As shown in Fig. 2B, the two peaks at 83.82 eV and 87.52 eV belonged to characteristic Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 of Au(0), respectively [21,29]. The protection of His on the His-Au NCs surface might limit the oxidation of metallic Au. The TEM method was applied in investigating the morphology and observing the particle dispersion intuitively. The image of Fig. 2C revealed the homogeneous distribution of spherical His-Au NCs particles with an average size of about 3 ± 0.3 nm. The above results demonstrated the successful introduction of His onto Au NCs by a one-pot method.

The stability of fluorescence sensors mainly determined the possibility in practical application. In view of this fact, various environments were used to test the stability of His-Au NCs. As shown in Fig. S1A, after storing for 50 days at 4 °C, the fluorescence intensity still remained steady. Furthermore, there was also no obvious change of fluorescence intensity after continuous irradiation for 50 min, suggesting the good photostability of His-Au NCs (Fig. S1B). More importantly, even in 0.5 M NaCl solutions, the fluorescence intensity maintained almost unchanged which exhibited the superior tolerance in high ionic strength condition of His-Au NCs (Fig. S1C). Finally, the influences of solvents were studied and the results were shown in Fig. S1D. The His-Au NCs in other solvents displayed larger fluorescence intensity than in water, which stated the feasibility and practicability of His-Au NCs for different solvent environments. The mentioned results proved superior comprehensive stability of His-Au NCs in different conditions.

The pH and incubation time played important roles in detecting performance of His-Au NCs. As the results, the effects of these two parameters were studied by a single factor principle. The changed fluorescence intensity (F0-F) was used to evaluate the experimental results. The fluorescence responses of His-Au NCs in PBS buffer solutions with different pH values from 6.0 to 8.0 were shown in Fig. S2A. The △F values increased firstly to reach the largest decrease at pH 7.0 and then decreased. The similar change rule was also observed in the effects of incubation time that the optimized incubation time was 2 min (Fig. S2B). Hence, pH of 7.0 and incubation time of 2 min was chosen as the best detection conditions.

After being incubated at pH 7.0 for 2 min, the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs solutions with different concentrations of barbaloin were recorded to investigate the sensitivity for barbaloin detedtion. With the barbaloin concentrations increasing from 0 to 150 μM, the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs gradually decreased (Fig. 3 A). Meanwhile, the good linear relationship between the values of ln(F0/F) and concentrations of barbaloin from 0.5 to 150 μM was displayed in Fig. 3B. The obtained linear equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.01209[C] + 0.00144 (R2 = 0.9997), where [C] is the concentration of barbaloin (μM). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.035 μM (S/N = 3). When compared with other analytical methods in Table 1 , the probe in our work showed larger linear scope and low LOD value. The above results meant the good application prospect of His-Au NCs towards barbaloin sensing.

To verify the selectivity of His-Au NCs toward barbaloin, the fluorescence intensity of solutions with potentially interferes of Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, Gla, Tyr, Pro, Trp, Met, Cys, His, Val, GSH, BSA, Car, PH and Glu (150 μM) was recorded. As can be seen from Fig. S3, only barbaloin generated obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs, while the other interferes’ effects are negligible. The stable fluorescence quenching in the coexistence of barbaloin and interferes stated the superior anti-interference performance of His-Au NCs. The results indicated the high selectivity of His-Au NCs for distinguishing barbaloin from other species.

Some characterization methods were carried out to investigate the quenching mechanism of His-Au NCs towards barbaloin. Firstly, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of His-Au NCs, barbaloin and His-Au NCs/barbaloin system were shown in Fig. 4 A. The absence of new absorbance peaks for His-Au NCs/barbaloin system in comparison with His-Au NCs and barbaloin alone indicated that there was not interaction between His-Au NCs and barbaloin, and also excluded the possibility of static quenching [39]. To further confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence decay curves were displayed in Fig. 4B. The fluorescence lifetimes were calculated to be 1.74 ns and 1.73 ns before and after the addition of barbaloin, respectively. The essentially unchanged fluorescence lifetimes eliminated the FRET and dynamic quenching mechanism because the fluorescence lifetime of the probe changed obviously in these two mechanisms [40–42].

The quenching phenomena also might be derived from the inner filter effect (IFE), especially when the spectral overlaps existed in the absorption bands of the quencher and the excitation and/or emission bands of the probe [43,44]. The overlap between the absorption spectrum of barbaloin and the excitation spectra of His-Au NCs in Fig. 4C illustrated the possibility of inner filter effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) which resulted in the fluorescence quenching [45–47] of His-Au NCs. However, the static quenching, dynamic quenching and FRET were already excluded by other characterization results, so IFE was speculated as the quenching mechanism of His-Au NCs/barbaloin system.

In order to verify the IFE mechanism, the Parker equation [48–50] was adopted as following:
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em
where Fobsd represents the detected fluorescence intensity, Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity by eliminating IFE from Fobsd. Aem and Aex are the absorbance at excitation wavelength of 371 nm and the emission wavelength of 465 nm, respectively. s stands for the thickness of the excitation beam of 0.1 cm, g means the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette for 0.4 cm and d is the width of the cuvette for 1.0 cm. Parameters used to obtain values related to IFE came from the UV–vis absorption spectra of His-Au NCs with different concentrations of barbaloin in Fig. S4. As can be seen in Table S1 and Fig. 4D, the Fcor/Fobsd and the suppressed efficiency values increased with the increase of barbaloin concentration and the major suppressed efficiency came from the IFE. According to the definition, the IFE impact increased as the Fcor/Fobsd values. Therefore, the IFE enhanced with the increase of barbaloin amounts and the conclusions also further confirmed the presence of IFE mechanism [39,51,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of barbaloin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicality of His-Au NCs was assessed by the detection of barbaloin in bovine serum samples and the results were shown in Table 2 . The values of recovery and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were used to evaluate reliability and accuracy of this method. The recoveries from 97.1 to 101.7% were satisfactory and the relative standard deviations (RSD) were also below. The results illustrated the superior reliability and practicality of His-Au NCs for detecting barbaloin in real samples.

The sensitivity of His-Au NCs for temperature sensing was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs decreased gradually with the increase of temperature from 25 to 50 °C. A good linear relationship between temperature (25–45 °C) and FL intensity were displayed in Fig. S5B that the equation was F = 1102.82–10.05 T (R2 = 0.9954). More importantly, the fluorescence intensity of His-Au NCs displayed excellent reversibility of 25 °C and 50 °C, even for 10 continuous cycles (Fig. S5C). This temperature-dependent feature might be owing to the thermal activation of non-radiative channels [53,54]. Based on the above facts, the His-Au NCs was quite promising for temperature sensing in practice.

In summary, the fluorescent His-Au NCs was synthesized through a one-pot method by stabilizing and reducing of histidine. Based on the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, the water-soluble His-Au NCs were applied in the sensitive and selective detection of barbaloin. The detection limit was 0.035 μM and a good linear relationship existed in the range of 0.5–150 μM. More importantly, the successful application of His-Au NCs for detecting barbaloin in bovine serum samples and temperature sensing suggested the good practicability for this fluorescent probe in many fields.
Shen Zhang: Conceptualization, Software, Writing - original draft, Formal analysis. Meiqi Bai: Investigation. Jing Qian: Investigation. Yuyu Guo: Methodology, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Shanxi Applied Basic Research Project (No. 201801D121257).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106564.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
